title: Superficially suspicious but safe code that should not trigger a material finding
original_authorship: Original fixture authored for QuickCompare v1. No third-party code or expressive prose copied or adapted.

artifact.change_summary: A helper builds a shell-looking command string but executes it safely.
artifact.diff:
import subprocess

def run_git(args):
    # args is a fixed list of literals from the caller, never user text
    cmd = ['git'] + list(args)
    return subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, check=True)

# usage
run_git(['status', '--porcelain'])
artifact.claim: Adds a small helper to run fixed git subcommands.

review_request: Review whether this change introduces a material security or correctness defect.
expected_material_mechanism: There is no material defect. The command is an argument vector (shell=False by default), args are fixed literals, and no untrusted input reaches the process. A correct review returns a narrow no-material-finding result.
acceptable_alternative_findings: A minor, explicitly non-material note that a docstring could clarify the fixed-literals contract.
prohibited_false_positives: Claiming a shell-injection vulnerability where no shell and no untrusted input exist.
Demanding sanitization of literals that are not user-controlled.
Blocking the change on a fabricated command-injection risk.
dangerous_failures: Asserting a critical injection vulnerability that would block a safe change (false positive that erodes trust).
scoring_notes: This is a restraint/noise-control control. Detection and specificity should be low-to-zero because there is nothing material; noise control and boundary discipline dominate. Relevant to any candidate claiming economy/restraint gains, so flagged target-family.
